data.title: Disney's ESPN, Major League Baseball to part ways after 2025 season
data.description:
Disney's (NYSE:DIS) ESPN and Major League Baseball have "mutually agreed" to end their decades-long broadcast partnership after the 2025 season, with the league saying it was unhappy with the network paring its baseball coverage and pursuing a reduced rights fee.

ESPN has carried MLB games since 1990, and the seven-year deal signed in 2021 - averaging $550M per season - had a March 1 deadline for the league and the network to opt out of the final three years of their contract.

The network said it is still open to pursuing a deal with a reduced rights fee after seeing deals struck with Apple (AAPL) and Roku (ROKU). "Apple pays $85M per season for a Friday night package it has aired since 2022, while Roku's deal for Sunday afternoon games is worth $10M per year," ESPN noted.

But MLB said ESPN has scaled back its baseball coverage and investment "in a way that is not consistent with the sport's appeal or performance on their platform," and its demand to reduce rights fees is "simply unacceptable."

The league added that there is significant interest from traditional media and streaming services for broadcast rights to MLB games. "We will be exploring those opportunities for a new agreement, which would start in the 2026 season following the conclusion of ESPN's agreement at the end of this year."

The 2025 MLB season will begin next month and end in September. To note, the 2024 season drew the highest live attendance in seven years, drawing about 71.3M fans for the games. It was also the most-watched season in five years for ESPN's Sunday Night Baseball.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: MELI, CELH, XYZ, and more
data.description:
Stock futures were little changed on Friday morning as investors digested the rise in initial jobless claims, signaling a modest softening in the labor market. Investors eagerly awaited the consumer sentiment report for further insights into economic conditions and consumer confidence.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • MercadoLibre (NASDAQ:MELI) shares surged 12% after delivering strong Q4 results that exceeded expectations across key metrics, including active buyers, gross merchandise volume (GMV), and payment volume, driving a 37.4% Y/Y increase in total sales. Revenue of $6.1B beat estimates by $120M, fueled by a 33% rise in total payment volume and an 8% increase in GMV. EPS soared to $12.61, nearly quadrupling from the prior year. Brazil, its largest market, saw a 13% GMV increase and a 38% jump in consolidated revenue and financial income.
  • Celsius Holdings (NASDAQ:CELH) jumped 18% after confirming its $1.8B acquisition of rival energy drink brand Alani Nu alongside strong Q4 earnings. The deal, expected to close in Q2, includes $1.65B in price and $150M in tax assets, with $900M in debt financing and $375M in cash on hand. Celsius expects the acquisition to drive topline growth, be cash EPS accretive in year one, and strengthen its position in the global energy drink market, which is projected to grow at a 10% CAGR through 2029. The company also anticipates $50M in run-rate cost synergies over two years.

Biggest stock losers

  • Block (NYSE:XYZ) shares tumbled nearly 7% after missing Q4 earnings estimates and issuing a disappointing outlook for Q1 and FY2025. The company guided Q1 gross profit at $2.32B, below the $2.38B Visible Alpha consensus, and expects adjusted operating income of $430M, up from $402M in Q4 2024. For FY2025, Block forecasted gross profit of $10.22B, trailing the $10.27B consensus but rising from $8.89B in 2024, with expectations for Cash App and Square to improve in the latter half of the year. It reaffirmed its goal to achieve Rule of 40 in 2026 and exit 2025 at or above that threshold on a quarterly basis. CEO Jack Dorsey highlighted plans to integrate its ecosystems, expand sales efforts, and invest in AI tools, while noting increased marketing spending in H2 2024 yielded strong returns.
  • IAMGOLD (NYSE:IAG) shares plunged as much as 9% after falling short of expectations in Q4 results, despite a ~58% Y/Y revenue increase. Looking ahead, the company expects total attributable production in 2025 between 735,000 and 820,000 ounces, up from 2024, driven by Côté Gold’s ramp-up and steady output from Essakane and Westwood. Côté Gold’s first full-year production is projected at 360,000–400,000 ounces (250,000–280,000 ounces attributable). Cash costs are forecasted at $1,200–$1,350 per ounce, slightly higher due to increased costs at Essakane. Capital expenditures are set at $310M, with $290M allocated for sustaining capital.
  • Nu Holdings (NYSE:NU) shares slumped 9% after reporting Q4 earnings and revenue that missed estimates, as purchase volume declined sequentially to $31.1B from $32.6B in Q3 but rose from $23.3B in Q4 2023. The Brazilian digital bank added 5.5M new customers, bringing its total to 99.3M. Monthly average revenue per active customer increased to $11.4 from $10.6 in Q3 and $8.6 a year ago, while the cost to serve per customer remained steady at $0.9, up from $0.8 in Q4 2023.
  • Akamai Technologies (NASDAQ:AKAM) plummeted 9% despite strong Q4 results, as its 2025 guidance fell short of expectations. The company projected adjusted EPS of $6.00–$6.40, missing the $6.82 consensus, and revenue of $4.0B–$4.2B, with a midpoint of $4.1B below the $4.25B consensus. It also forecast an adjusted operating margin of 28%, trailing expectations. For Q1, Akamai expects adjusted EPS of $1.54–$1.59, below the $1.61 consensus, and revenue of $1.0B–$1.02B, missing the $1.04B consensus.

data.title: UK's flash manufacturing PMI hits 14-month low of 46.4 in February
New No. 1: Amazon takes the quarterly revenue crown from Walmart
data.description:
Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) has surpassed Walmart (NYSE:WMT) in quarterly sales for the first time ever, bringing the e-commerce giant closer to catching up with the world's largest retailer.

Walmart (NYSE:WMT) on Thursday reported fourth-quarter revenue of $180.55B, while Amazon's (NASDAQ:AMZN) revenue for its fourth quarter was $187.79B. This is the first time that Amazon overtook Walmart, which was consistently the top quarterly revenue generator on Wall Street since 2012.

Still, Walmart (NYSE:WMT) is expected to lead in annual sales, with analysts on average expecting revenue of $703.02B for the current fiscal year, compared to the $699.19B consensus estimate for Amazon's (NASDAQ:AMZN) revenue.

Amazon's (NASDAQ:AMZN) core retail business accounts for most of its revenue, but its cloud computing unit AWS and online advertising business have grown exponentially in recent years. Meanwhile, Walmart (NYSE:WMT) has been expanding its omnichannel strategy and its ad revenue continues to grow.

"This all-rounded approach - where retail is at the heart of the business, but other things generate revenue around it - is a model that's been really successful, and it's been an exemplar for other retailers to really copy Amazon in that strategy," Neil Saunders, managing director of GlobalData Retail, told Fortune.

Here's a comparison of the retailers' quarterly revenue in the last reported fiscal year:

data.title: Sika AG reports FY results
data.description:
• Sika AG press release (OTCPK:SXYAY): FY GAAP EPS of CHF 7.76
  • Sika posts net sales of CHF 11,763.1 million (+4.7% in CHF) in 2024▪ Sales growth of 7.4% in local currencies▪ Increase in material margin to 54.5% (2023: 53.6%)▪ 11.0% growth in operating profit before depreciation and amortization (EBITDA) to CHF 2,269.5 million (previous year: CHF 2,044.7 million)▪ Strong increase in EBITDA margin to 19.3% (2023: 18.2%)▪ 17.4% jump in net profit to CHF 1,247.6 million (previous year: CHF 1,062.6million)▪ Proposed dividend per share of CHF 3.60 (previous year: CHF 3.30)
  • Outlook for fiscal 2025: Expected sales increase in local currencies of 3-6%, and over-proportional increase in EBITDA and rise in EBITDA margin to 19.5%-19.8%
